speaker: interviewee
text: So that Karli Oruslan, who wore a star, slept with me very frequently. When I say slept with me, I mean he s-- slept in my home. One evening I come home and here Karlie Oruslan lies in bed. His face is as white as the sheet. I say, Karli, what happened? He says, I blew the cover. I say, what do you mean? He says, when I came in from street in the lobby of the apartment, I had to sneeze. So I went into my pocket, I took out my handkerchief, put my handkerchief to my nose and I sneezed, and a goddamn yellow star came out of the handkerchief and fell right on the spot on my jacket where it was supposed to be. And the landlady looked at me in disdain. The next morning, the landlady came to me and she said to me, 'Peter, I have known all the time that you are part of the Dutch underground, [INAUDIBLE] underground things, and I applaud you for it. I have nothing against Jews. Nothing at all. But you know the penalty if I help Jews is death, in most cases. I come to a concentration camp where I will get killed, and I don't dare to help Jews. You have to promise me that you never bring a Jew here.'

speaker: interviewer
text: I promised. I never kept my promise. It has been bothering me for a long time, philosophically. Do I have the right to expose someone to the possible penalty of death for taking-- making her take a-- a risk which she is unaware of and is not willing to do? Do I have that right? It bothered me tremendously.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one rabbi and one Catholic priest, whom I talked to separately-- I did this after the war, by the way-- both gave me the same answer, and it satisfied me. And-- and they said as follows. If you do it to save your own life, it is wrong. But if you do it to save someone else's life, it will be excused. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I think that is very interesting point of view. And it-- it bothered me. And that happened all the time. Because all the people who helped me-- lots of people-- most of them did not know I was Jewish. And the same is true for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most [INAUDIBLE], I mean underground people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eric, while you were underground, you met your wife, Edith.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us about Edith and-- and the work that she did with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-- Edith lived in a community about 50 kilometers out of-- out of Amsterdam. And someone who was fairly close to her was transported-- was in a transport train to Westerbork. And he smuggled a letter out of the train. And it-- and the letter came to me through the people who smuggled it, and they said to me, here is a letter for such and such person. If you could please go on your goddamn bicycle and bring it to her. I say, I got to read the letter first. No, this person say. I don't care. I have to read it. I read the letter. I don't want to go into what was in there. I went on my bike. I went to that little community where she lived. I gave her the letter, and it was very sad. And she said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't stand here any longer. She has been-- was underground at-- at some farmer's where she was milking cows, than she was now in Bilthoven in the family where she was working as a maid. Nobody knew she was Jewish. Then she came a few weeks later to Amsterdam, and we have been together ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was she doing during the war? She was basically the distributor of that money which I was talking about from the Dutch government. And she-- and she wore a uniform of a Red Cross. Sis-- What do you call it? Red Cross? Oh, oh, the Red Cross nurse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nurse. Red Cross nurse. And she brought food around and she went with another elderly, non-Jewish lady many times on trips to Friesland to get food. You could put in a bicycle maybe 20 to 30 pounds of food, so on two bicycles, 50 to 60. It is much later because, in the winter 1944, people practically starved to death. You only could live on the black market. And she also had underground-- underground places, especially at one Jew-- Jewish lady was a piano player for the [INAUDIBLE] orchestra. And was very, very interesting when the lady, where my wife at the time, who was not my wife at the time, lived there. She said, well, you have to take care of her. She looks so Jewish. I don't want to-- to walk on the street with her. So Edith went to that lady and took her from one place to another. And the-- her landlady said, I don't want to be on the street with her. She looks too Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when the war was over? But before the war was over, your son Michael was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was born. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came-- came to a Dutch [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] family for about half a year. And then he came into a orphanage for retarded children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was only-- one very cute thing happened. Because you see, the wonderful thing is, during all the tragedy, funny things are happening. The war was over. Michael did not have an official certificate of birth. So I got my stamp karte for-- for food distribution, and so did his mother. But I couldn't get one for him because he had no birth certificate. So I went to distribution center.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he? '43, '45. A little bit over two years. And I said, well, here is his name is such and such, and he is Jewish boy. He is born underground. There is no birth certificate, and I need a card for him to get food rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, no birth certificate, no food rations. So I-- Michael was in my arm. I took him off my arm. I put it on his desk and said, and you feed him. Bye bye. Come back! He-- [LAUGHTER] You feed him. I think it was funny. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From a personal point of view, I can say only one thing. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] When the war was over and Canadian troops entered Holland, like we have seen about Paris ON television and ra-- and-- and-- and movie all the time, there was a giant celebration. People were dancing and laughing and every corner was a-- was a orchestra. Don't forget, we talk about spring. It was a beautiful spring day. And these Dutch girls, they were hugging and kissing with the soldiers. And everybody was happy. And Edith and I, we wanted to dance also and partake in the festivities. But we were standing there and we were crying. And we were crying maybe the first time in years. Because it was at that moment [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that all our friends who got killed and didn't make it were with us. And we could not enjoy the day of-- or that evening of celebration. It was maybe one of the worst evenings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Another terrible experience I had. I'm-- I'm tracking back now. I was visiting a guy that was very early, just very shortly after we went underground. And that guy, he was also an [INAUDIBLE] of the Federation Council. And he was safe from deportation. And I visited with him one evening, and his father lived with him. And was in summer, obviously. Must've been August, '42, maybe September. So a car came, stopped in front of the apartment, and police came out. The Grune Polizei, the Green Police, the German police in Holland. And they came up and the guy said to me, oh, I don't know. Go in the bathroom, hide. I go in the bathroom and-- and was hiding. The police-- two policeman came up and they asked where is-- I forgot the name-- they asked for his father. And the old man was not safe from deportation, like his son, who was an [INAUDIBLE] of the Federation Council. So they asked for his father, and the old man came. And I didn't see him. I just could listen out of the bathroom. And he was whining [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a little-- like a little baby. Not like a dog. And it was just terrible to listen to that. I will never forget the voice of that man. So it's the little things. But they're still there, and they always will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eric, how do you-- how do you deal with the pain you endured all those years now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had four [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] years, maybe two or three years, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at least once a week nightmares where I-- nightmares. And then it became less and less and less. I have no-- I still have nightmares today occasionally, but about other things as well. I-- I deal with it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's-- it's a part of me. I would say my-- my experience in the Dutch underground is the most important forming element of my life, maybe even more than my-- my parents. Maybe second to them. And I'm still very emotional about it and I live with it every day. And I do everything-- I don't suffer anymore because somehow I feel I have done my best, and I never can be as good as I was during those years. Whatever I do today is-- is nothing. I was a very good boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your exp--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it makes me feel good. So I have [INAUDIBLE] feeling. I think of everybody who's dead, but to also say, well, what a wonderful guy was I, and how goddamn lucky. Why was-- I-- I don't know. I mean, there is no point in going into-- I could talk for hours about things happened. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was the one girl, Nell van Aecker. She was terrific, Dutch girl. Not a Jewish girl, of course. And the daughter of that-- [INAUDIBLE] of-- well, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you. How did your experience change you? How did your experience in the war and the underground change you? Or did it change you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to repeat what I was before, because I was a spoiled, self centered, egoistic, well to do, rotten, Jewish boy. And the underground changed me-- hopefully I'm right what I'm going to say now-- in a mature, caring human being who still makes bad things once in a while. But basically I'm mature and caring and I like myself. I also-- I-- I'm serious now. I also do believe that my involvement-- I never use the word charity, by the way. Charity is a very bad word. Charity is if there's an earthquake in Honduras and we give some money. That is charity. But to take care of your own people in your own Jewish community in Los Angeles, or in our own country, in America, we have responsibilities, which is not charity. And I am very much convinced that my involvement in activity and financially [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is based on the fact that the-- my underground years gave me a feeling of responsibility. I believe that is the big change. I might have matured anyway, but I will never know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you first came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to America with $4,000 in my pocket, which was, at that time, an awful lot of money. Saved from the black market in Holland after the war, selling cigarettes. I'm not ashamed. And I came here. I didn't like the country at all. Not at all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I've spent a lot of money for the [INAUDIBLE], I mean the ship's fare. And I said, well, I got terrible time after me. Don't forget I was involved in war crime trials after the war as a witness in Holland. And I said, well, I have to settle for good time. And I spent $2,000 between June and October. Was a lot of money but I lived like a king. And my cousin, who was my-- my sponsor, always said to me, Eric, you got to work. You got to work. I say, did I ask you for any money? No. Till I ask you for money, you shut up, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: In October, I took a job. And then I started to like the country. I had a wonderful job. I don't want to go into that. And Edith lived in Israel at the time to see her parents, who survived the war in Romania. In the meantime, Edith came to America about a year later and then we both moved to California. We came to California in July '48, and to America in June '47. I did lots of things here. I was working, not too hard. But we got very lucky. Edith and I, we were wonderful combination, also in our business, which we started in 1956. And I was working as a traveling salesman from '48 to '56, and she was working as a fashion designer. And in '56, we went in business together. Life was very good to us. But then in the early '60s, around 1960, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was thinking an awful lot of my parents and my home. And we were very outstanding-- not outstanding. Not prominent. We were a family of reputation in Heidelberg. And I said, I am nobody, nobody here in-- in Los Angeles. And I became heavily involved, many times as a president or chairman or in leadership position in many organizations. And that was done [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of feeling of responsibility, but also, to a very large degree, to satisfy my desire that I am more than the latest apparel manufacturer. It was very important to me to be somebody, and maybe I am. I was involved in so many things. Today I'm too old. Nobody wants me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But everybody still loves you. You did so many wonderful things. Everybody knows you, Eric.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have-- I was-- I mean, [INAUDIBLE] it's not part of our conversation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: The things I did here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you were very-- and you told me you were very involved in the Jewish Federation and many Jewish organizations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I was president, for three years, of the Merchant's Club downtown. I was president for two and one, for three years, of the California Fashion Creators, which is trade organization of the apparel industry. I was chairman of the apparel division of the UJF. Then I was chairman of the Finance Committee. I was chairman of the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] New Gifts Committee. I was a member of the Executive Council. I was a vice president. I was vice president of the City of Hope. Chairman of many committees. Was a chairman of the National Conference of Christians and Jews. And blah, blah, blah. And it makes me feel good to say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I knew you were familiar. [LAUGHTER] With all the good you did. Let me ask you a serious question. Do you have a message that you would like to leave for your son, Michael, your grandson, Joshua, and the rest of the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: As recently as last week, I wrote a letter to my son, sealed, to be opened after my death. And in that letter, I wrote a little speech, exactly 90 seconds, which I want someone-- hopefully my son-- to say at my funeral. It's going to be very, very funny. The message I have to my son and to my grandson and to anybody else-- two things are important. As Jews, we should not think of us only as survivors, because if you do-- don't do anything else but survive, you didn't do anything. You have to make the best of it, and it got to give you a feeling of responsibility and you got to live up to that. That I say as a Jew. As a human being, my message to my son and my grandson will be, always fight for what is right. Don't let anyone step on you. Be-- don't. Unless someone's life is at stake, stand up for what is right. Don't eat anybody's banana peels, and stand up for what is right and fight it. And when you are right, you're going to win. And be tolerant, and don't lie. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Steal is better than lying. I think the lie is a terrible thing because it degrades yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to add-- that's a wonderful message-- before we finish? Any last words you--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what is [INAUDIBLE]. Shall I say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We Jews, in my opinion, make a terrible mistake because when we talk about the Holocaust, we talk almost exclusively about us. Us Jews. Which is understandable, because we lost our parents, we lost our children, we lost our friends and brothers. We are emotionally involved with our own people, as we should be. However, the Nazis did not only kill six million Jews, but 10 to 12 million other people. And I'm not talking about soldiers. Three million Russian prisoners of war were killed in concentration camps with the Jews. Of course they were only men. The gypsies were destroyed in family units, just like us. If we keep on talking about the Holocaust as a Jewish catastrophe-- which it was, but we talk about it exclusively-- we miss the message to the world. Because the 18-year-old gasoline station attendant in Kenosha, Wisconsin has never seen a Jew in his life, probably. And if he hears about the Holocaust of the Jews, he says, I haven't got anything to do with it. We must understand and realize that what happened under the Nazi regime in Europe is a human catastrophe. And we-- not we Jews, we human beings-- can only learn from it if we realize it is not a Jewish catastrophe only. Which does not take a great effect that emotionally it is-- the Jews are the most important thing to us. But intellectually and historically, we have to talk about the others also. That's my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for opening up your home and your heart, telling us your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean I didn't open my mouth? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of that. Thank you, seriously.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I do hope-- we are not on tape anymore?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do hope that that last remark which I made will be considered by the Foundation, or by whoever is listening to the tape, in the spirit in which it is meant. It is not criticism. It is-- I'm begging everybody to see the things like they were, and like they are, and let's learn from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture on the right says, E. S. Flegenheimer, Erich Simon Flegenheimer. When the Germans-- the Nazis-- invaded Holland in May, 1940, in summer, July or August '40, they required everybody to have a personal ID. Jews had to-- needed the letter J, for Jew, on their ID card. The card on the left says Peter Feenstra. That is my false ID when I went underground. And that picture was taken in July or August, 1942. That's when I went underground. That is the picture of my parents at their wedding invitation, which was in 1910. I lost both my parents at the age of 54. My father died in 1929, 54. My mother died in 1941, age 54.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Isidore Flegenheimer. My mother's name was Rosa Flegenheimer, born Leiter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was this taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Germany, at their wedding. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That adorable young child on the left is me, and my estimated age is about two or three. At the center is my wonderful mother. On the right is my beautiful and great sister, Ruth, who also died at the age of 54 in 1960 or '61 or '62 in Davos, Switzerland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Looking at this picture makes it impossible for me to ever again look into the mirror. That is me at the age of approximately 16 or 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Neckar River in Heidelberg. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That is the picture of my adorable and caring daughter-in-law, Cindy, and my son, Michael, who claims that he is my greatest accomplishment. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Picture was taken about 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles, California. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is the picture of my amazing, amusing, and promising grandson, Joshua Flagg. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That is a recent picture of my wonderful wife, Edith, also known as [INAUDIBLE], and me. And the picture was taken in 1994 at the Hollywood Bowl in Los Angeles.
